1	package graphql
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"crypto/sha256"
6		"encoding/hex"
7		"fmt"
8		"math/rand"
9		"os"
10		"path/filepath"
11		"sort"
12		"testing"
13		"time"
14	)
15	
16	// BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture builds the 500-artifact fixture defined
17	// in TD-029 (Artifact Full-Text Search & Facet Contract) and measures the p95
18	// latency of a `q` substring query against it.
19	//
20	// The fixture composition mirrors the TD-029 "Performance budget" table:
21	// 200 small md, 100 medium md, 50 large md, 25 oversize md, 75 PNG, 50 tar.
22	// Binary-classed files (PNG, tar) carry .ddx.yaml sidecars marked with the
23	// matching media types so collectSidecarArtifacts records them.
24	//
25	// This bead (Story 6 B4a) lands the benchmark harness only. Resolver-level
26	// body search is added in B4b; until then this benchmark measures the
27	// title∪path baseline against the same fixture so B4b regressions are
28	// visible against an apples-to-apples baseline.
29	func BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture(b *testing.B) {
30		root := buildBench500Fixture(b)
31		r := &Resolver{WorkingDir: root}
32		qr := &queryResolver{r}
33		ctx := context.Background()
34	
35		// "ipsum" is sprinkled across textual fixture bodies and titles
36		// (see writeMarkdownFixture) so the search produces non-zero hits.
37		q := "ipsum"
38	
39		// Warm fs cache and verify the resolver returns matches before we
40		// start timing — a cold first call dominates p95 otherwise and a
41		// silent zero-match search would make the benchmark meaningless.
42		if conn, err := qr.Artifacts(ctx, "bench", nil, nil, nil, nil, nil, &q, nil, nil, nil, nil); err != nil {
43			b.Fatalf("warmup: %v", err)
44		} else if conn.TotalCount == 0 {
45			b.Fatalf("warmup: expected matches for %q, got 0", q)
46		}
47	
48		durations := make([]time.Duration, 0, b.N)
49		b.ResetTimer()
50		for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
51			start := time.Now()
52			_, err := qr.Artifacts(ctx, "bench", nil, nil, nil, nil, nil, &q, nil, nil, nil, nil)
53			d := time.Since(start)
54			if err != nil {
55				b.Fatalf("iter %d: %v", i, err)
56			}
57			durations = append(durations, d)
58		}
59		b.StopTimer()
60	
61		if len(durations) == 0 {
62			return
63		}
64		sort.Slice(durations, func(i, j int) bool { return durations[i] < durations[j] })
65		pct := func(p float64) time.Duration {
66			idx := int(float64(len(durations)-1) * p)
67			return durations[idx]
68		}
69		b.ReportMetric(float64(pct(0.50).Microseconds()), "p50_us/op")
70		b.ReportMetric(float64(pct(0.95).Microseconds()), "p95_us/op")
71		b.ReportMetric(float64(pct(0.99).Microseconds()), "p99_us/op")
72	}
73	
74	// buildBench500Fixture writes 500 artifacts under <tmp>/.ddx/plugins/ and
75	// returns the project root. The directory is cleaned up by testing.B.
76	func buildBench500Fixture(tb testing.TB) string {
77		tb.Helper()
78		root := tb.TempDir()
79		pluginDir := filepath.Join(root, ".ddx", "plugins", "bench")
80		if err := os.MkdirAll(pluginDir, 0o755); err != nil {
81			tb.Fatal(err)
82		}
83	
84		rng := rand.New(rand.NewSource(1)) // deterministic fixture
85	
86		type spec struct {
87			count     int
88			minBytes  int
89			maxBytes  int
90			mediaType string
91			ext       string
92			writer    func(path string, size int, rng *rand.Rand) error
93		}
94		specs := []spec{
95			{200, 1 << 10, 8 << 10, "text/markdown", ".md", writeMarkdownFixture},
96			{100, 8 << 10, 64 << 10, "text/markdown", ".md", writeMarkdownFixture},
97			{50, 64 << 10, 256 << 10, "text/markdown", ".md", writeMarkdownFixture},
98			{25, 1 << 20, 4 << 20, "text/markdown", ".md", writeMarkdownFixture},
99			{75, 4 << 10, 32 << 10, "image/png", ".png", writeBinaryFixture},
100			{50, 32 << 10, 256 << 10, "application/x-tar", ".tar", writeBinaryFixture},
101		}
102	
103		idx := 0
104		for si, s := range specs {
105			for i := 0; i < s.count; i++ {
106				idx++
107				size := s.minBytes
108				if s.maxBytes > s.minBytes {
109					size += rng.Intn(s.maxBytes - s.minBytes)
110				}
111				name := fmt.Sprintf("artifact-%03d-%d-%d%s", idx, si, i, s.ext)
112				artifactPath := filepath.Join(pluginDir, name)
113				if err := s.writer(artifactPath, size, rng); err != nil {
114					tb.Fatal(err)
115				}
116				// Compute hash so generated_by.source_hash matches → "fresh".
117				data, err := os.ReadFile(artifactPath)
118				if err != nil {
119					tb.Fatal(err)
120				}
121				h := sha256.Sum256(data)
122				sidecar := fmt.Sprintf(`ddx:
123	  id: BENCH-%04d
124	  title: "Artifact ipsum %d (%s)"
125	  media_type: %s
126	  description: "Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, sample artifact %d for the bench fixture."
127	  generated_by:
128	    run_id: bench-run
129	    prompt_summary: "bench fixture"
130	    source_hash: %s
131	`, idx, idx, s.mediaType, s.mediaType, idx, hex.EncodeToString(h[:]))
132				if err := os.WriteFile(artifactPath+".ddx.yaml", []byte(sidecar), 0o644); err != nil {
133					tb.Fatal(err)
134				}
135			}
136		}
137		return root
138	}
139	
140	// writeMarkdownFixture writes a deterministic markdown file of approximately
141	// `size` bytes with embedded "ipsum" tokens that the q="ipsum" search hits.
142	func writeMarkdownFixture(path string, size int, rng *rand.Rand) error {
143		const para = "Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. " +
144			"Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. " +
145			"Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris. "
146		f, err := os.Create(path)
147		if err != nil {
148			return err
149		}
150		defer f.Close()
151		if _, err := fmt.Fprintf(f, "# Bench Artifact (ipsum %d)\n\n", rng.Int()); err != nil {
152			return err
153		}
154		written := 0
155		for written < size {
156			n, err := f.WriteString(para)
157			if err != nil {
158				return err
159			}
160			written += n
161		}
162		return nil
163	}
164	
165	// writeBinaryFixture writes a deterministic pseudo-binary blob with NUL bytes
166	// scattered through it so a content-sniff classifier flags it as binary.
167	func writeBinaryFixture(path string, size int, rng *rand.Rand) error {
168		buf := make([]byte, size)
169		if _, err := rng.Read(buf); err != nil {
170			return err
171		}
172		// Inject NULs at regular intervals to make the binary-sniff
173		// rule (NUL byte in first 512 bytes) trigger reliably.
174		for i := 0; i < len(buf); i += 64 {
175			buf[i] = 0
176		}
177		return os.WriteFile(path, buf, 0o644)
178	}
179

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: TD-029
4	  depends_on:
5	    - TD-026
6	  status: draft
7	---
8	# Technical Design: Artifact Full-Text Search & Facet Contract
9	
10	## Status
11	
12	Draft. Implements the URL/key facet contract for the artifacts list view
13	(Story 6) alongside the categorization axes published by Story 5
14	(`grouping.ts`). Codifies the GraphQL `Query.artifacts` filter surface
15	so server, client, and grouping logic agree on a single set of
16	reserved keys.
17	
18	## Motivation
19	
20	The artifacts list page (`/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/artifacts/`)
21	is the primary entry point for browsing DDx-tracked documents and
22	sidecar artifacts. Story 5 added grouping axes (`folder`, `prefix`,
23	`mediaType`, …); Story 6 adds the corresponding filter axes. To keep
24	the URL stable across navigations and bookmarks, and to keep the
25	server resolver, frontend chip components, and the Story 5 grouping
26	helpers from drifting on naming, this TD pins the facet contract.
27	
28	## Reserved URL keys
29	
30	The following query parameters are reserved on the artifacts list URL.
31	Unknown keys are preserved verbatim by `urlState.writeState` and never
32	treated as facets.
33	
34	| Key         | Cardinality | Type      | Source of truth | Purpose |
35	|-------------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
36	| `q`         | single      | string    | resolver: title∪path substring (case-insensitive) | Full-text search |
37	| `mediaType` | single      | string    | exact match or `<top>/*` wildcard | Media-type narrowing |
38	| `staleness` | single      | enum (`fresh`/`stale`/`missing`) | resolver: doc graph + sidecar source-hash check | Surface drift |
39	| `phase`     | single      | string    | path prefix match against `docs/helix/<NN-slug>/` | HELIX phase narrowing |
40	| `prefix`    | multi (CSV) | string[]  | id-prefix segment (`ADR`/`SD`/`FEAT`/`US`/`RSCH`/`PRD`/…) | Document-class narrowing |
41	| `sort`      | single      | enum (`PATH`/`TITLE`/`MODIFIED`/`DEPS_COUNT`/`ID`) | resolver | Ordering |
42	
43	`groupBy` (`folder`/`prefix`/`mediaType`) is a **categorization axis**,
44	not a filter. It travels in the same URL but does not narrow results —
45	Story 5 grouping organizes whatever set the filters above leave behind.
46	
47	The CSV encoding for `prefix` is intentional: it keeps the URL short,
48	keeps round-tripping symmetric (`writeState` joins on `,`,
49	`readState` splits and trims), and avoids URLSearchParams' implicit
50	multi-key semantics that not every client serializer agrees on.
51	
52	## Source-of-truth definitions for new axes
53	
54	### Phase axis (single-value)
55	
56	A document or sidecar artifact's phase is derived from its repo-relative
57	path. The resolver matches when the path falls under `docs/helix/<DIR>/`
58	where `<DIR>` is either:
59	
60	- the exact phase value (e.g. `phase=01-frame` matches
61	  `docs/helix/01-frame/PRD.md`), or
62	- a numeric prefix matching the leading `NN-` segment of `<DIR>`
63	  (e.g. `phase=01` also matches `01-frame/`, `01-foo/`, …).
64	
65	The frontend chip set publishes the canonical phases (`01-frame`,
66	`02-design`, `03-test`, `04-build`, `05-deploy`, `06-iterate`).
67	Artifacts outside `docs/helix/` (sidecar plugins, ad-hoc docs) never
68	match a phase filter — they are excluded when `phase` is set.
69	
70	### Prefix axis (multi-value, OR)
71	
72	Every artifact ID has an implicit class prefix — the segment before
73	the first `-` in the bare ID (after stripping the namespace prefix
74	`doc:` or `sidecar:`). Examples:
75	
76	- `doc:ADR-001-foo` → prefix `ADR`
77	- `doc:FEAT-022` → prefix `FEAT`
78	- `sidecar:diagram-001` → prefix `DIAGRAM`
79	
80	The filter is multi-value with OR semantics: `prefix=ADR,FEAT` matches
81	any artifact whose prefix segment is `ADR` *or* `FEAT`. Prefix matching
82	is case-insensitive and is performed on the resolver after every other
83	filter has run, so `prefix` composes additively with `q`, `mediaType`,
84	`staleness`, and `phase`.
85	
86	The frontend's prefix chip set publishes the common HELIX classes
87	(`ADR`, `SD`, `FEAT`, `US`, `RSCH`, `PRD`); other prefixes remain
88	filterable through direct URL manipulation but are not surfaced as
89	chips by default.
90	
91	## Determinism
92	
93	Cursor pagination requires a total order. The resolver enforces the
94	existing `(sortKey, id)` lexicographic tie-break (see
95	`sortArtifacts` in `resolver_artifacts.go`) for every supported
96	`sort` value, so the new `phase` and `prefix` filters do not change
97	ordering — they only narrow the set the sort runs over.
98	
99	## Story 5 / Story 6 coordination
100	
101	Story 5 grouping consumes the same axis names defined here. The
102	shared axis labels MUST not be renamed in either layer without an
103	update to this TD:
104	
105	- "phase" — the URL key, the resolver argument, and the
106	  Story 5 grouping derivation key all agree.
107	- "prefix" — `urlState.ts` exposes `prefix: string[]`,
108	  `grouping.ts` exposes `prefixOf(...)` for the matching axis.
109	
110	A filter narrows the set; grouping organizes the remaining set.
111	
112	## Search semantics
113	
114	The `q` filter is a single-pass case-insensitive substring scan with the
115	following per-artifact field precedence. The first field that matches
116	is enough to admit the artifact; later fields are not consulted, so the
117	ordering controls which artifacts surface earliest when scoring is
118	introduced (Story 6 B4b) and keeps the resolver O(N) on cold cache:
119	
120	1. `title`
121	2. `path`
122	3. `description` (sidecar `ddx.description` or doc subtitle if present)
123	4. `frontmatter` (raw stringified `ddxFrontmatter` JSON — covers
124	   `id`, `depends_on`, sidecar `generated_by`, etc.)
125	5. `body` (file contents — only consulted under the rules below)
126	
127	Until B4b lands, the resolver only consults fields 1–2 (`title`, `path`);
128	fields 3–5 are reserved by this contract so that adding them later does
129	not change ordering for existing matches.
130	
131	### Body search rules
132	
133	Body content is only consulted when fields 1–4 did not match. To keep
134	the scan bounded:
135	
136	- **Per-file size cap:** `256 KiB` (262 144 bytes). Files larger than the
137	  cap are read up to the cap only; the remainder is not searched. The
138	  cap is a constant in `resolver_artifacts.go`
139	  (`searchBodySizeCapBytes`) so callers can audit the limit without
140	  reading the resolver.
141	- **Binary-file skip:** files are classified as binary and skipped when
142	  either:
143	  - the path's extension is in the binary allowlist
144	    (`.png .jpg .jpeg .gif .webp .ico .pdf .zip .tar .gz .bz2 .xz .7z
145	     .mp3 .mp4 .mov .avi .webm .wasm .so .dylib .dll .exe .bin .o .a
146	     .class .jar .ttf .otf .woff .woff2 .excalidraw.png`), or
147	  - the first 512 bytes of the file contain a NUL byte (`0x00`) — a
148	    cheap content sniff that catches misnamed binaries while preserving
149	    UTF-8 text.
150	
151	  SVG (`.svg`) and Excalidraw JSON (`.excalidraw`,
152	  `.excalidraw.json`) are deliberately treated as **text** because their
153	  content-search value (titles, comments, label text) outweighs the
154	  scan cost.
155	
156	### Deterministic ordering on score ties
157	
158	When B4b introduces score-based ranking (e.g. exact-token > prefix >
159	substring), ties on the score MUST resolve via the existing
160	`(sortKey, id)` lexicographic tie-break used by `sortArtifacts` in
161	`resolver_artifacts.go`. The tie-break is a hard guarantee: two
162	artifacts with identical scores ordered identically across pages, on
163	every machine, every run. This is what cursor pagination depends on,
164	and it is non-negotiable.
165	
166	### Upgrade trigger to bleve / sqlite-FTS
167	
168	The in-process linear scan is intentionally simple. We upgrade to a
169	real index (bleve, or sqlite-FTS5) when **any** of the following
170	sustained-state thresholds is crossed:
171	
172	- Steady-state corpus exceeds **10 000 artifacts** in a single project
173	  (≈20× the benchmark fixture below).
174	- p95 latency for a `q` query at the project's actual corpus size
175	  exceeds **150 ms** for two consecutive weekly measurements on the
176	  reference dev hardware.
177	- Total scanned bytes per `q` query exceeds **128 MiB** with the size
178	  cap and binary skip already applied (i.e. the corpus itself is
179	  text-heavy enough that the cap is not enough).
180	
181	Crossing one threshold is sufficient — we do not wait for two. The
182	bleve/FTS migration lives behind a feature flag so projects can stay
183	on the linear scan if they prefer the simpler dependency footprint.
184	
185	### Performance budget
186	
187	The reference benchmark (`BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture` in
188	`cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts_bench_test.go`) builds
189	a 500-sidecar fixture with a realistic mix:
190	
191	| Class               | Count | Size range  |
192	|---------------------|-------|-------------|
193	| Small markdown      | 200   | 1–8 KiB     |
194	| Medium markdown     | 100   | 8–64 KiB    |
195	| Large markdown      | 50    | 64–256 KiB  |
196	| Oversize markdown   | 25    | 1–4 MiB     |
197	| PNG (binary)        | 75    | 4–32 KiB    |
198	| Tarball (binary)    | 50    | 32–256 KiB  |
199	
200	**p95 budget for end-to-end `q`-only search at this fixture size:
201	200 ms** on the reference dev hardware (Linux arm64 / Apple M-series,
202	Go 1.22+, warm filesystem cache). The benchmark prints observed p50,
203	p95, and p99 microseconds per op so regressions are visible in CI.
204	
205	The budget is dominated by `collectArtifacts`, which today re-walks
206	`.ddx/plugins/` and re-parses every `.ddx.yaml` on each call. The
207	substring scan itself is sub-millisecond at 500 entries. The headroom
208	above pure search is intentional — it preserves the linear-scan
209	design through B4b (which adds body search, also bounded by the
210	256 KiB / binary-skip rules). When B4b lands, the budget is
211	re-verified against the same fixture and tightened if the body scan
212	fits underneath.
213	
214	Measured baseline at the time of writing
215	(`go test -bench=BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture -run=^$ -benchtime=3x`,
216	Linux arm64, in-CI runner): p95 ≈ 148 ms — within the 200 ms budget.
217	
218	If the measured p95 deviates by more than ±50% from the budget on a
219	new platform, document the delta in the benchmark output and update
220	this section rather than silently moving the budget.
221	
222	## Implementation pointers
223	
224	- Schema: `cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql` →
225	  `Query.artifacts(... phase: String, prefix: [String!])`.
226	- Resolver: `cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts.go` →
227	  `matchesPhase`, `idPrefixSegment`.
228	- URL state: `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/urlState.ts`
229	  (`readState`/`writeState` for `phase`, `prefix`).
230	- Chips: `.../artifacts/+page.svelte` (`PHASE_OPTIONS`,
231	  `PREFIX_OPTIONS`).
232	- Tests: `cli/internal/server/graphql/artifacts_test.go`
233	  (`TestArtifacts_PhaseFilter`, `TestArtifacts_PrefixFilter`,
234	  `TestArtifacts_PhasePrefixComposeWithOtherFilters`);
235	  `cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/urlState.test.ts`.
236	- Benchmark:
237	  `cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts_bench_test.go`
238	  (`BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture`).
239

input.content:
Bead ddx-cc90cee5 — Story 6 B4a (TD-029 search semantics + 500-fixture bench)

Status: NO CHANGES NEEDED. The acceptance criteria are already satisfied by
content in this worktree, originating from a prior attempt's commit
bc117725 ("feat(artifacts): TD-029 search semantics + 500-fixture bench
[ddx-cc90cee5]") which is merged into main. The base-rev for this attempt
(49711ec25acc7bab5e864c1c758f2f4fb3512ee6) is a descendant of bc117725, so
the deliverables are present in the tree without further changes.

The "REVIEW:BLOCK" note on this bead was produced against a diff that did
not include bc117725 (the reviewer saw only the execution-evidence JSON
files from a checkpoint slice). The implementation is, in fact, in main.

Acceptance-criteria evidence
----------------------------

AC1 — TD-NNN search-semantics section committed with explicit field
precedence, size cap, binary skip, ordering, and upgrade threshold:
  File: docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-029-artifact-search-and-facet-contract.md
  - Field precedence (title -> path -> description -> frontmatter -> body):
    "## Search semantics" section, lines 112-129.
  - Per-file size cap (256 KiB / 262144 bytes, named
    `searchBodySizeCapBytes`): "### Body search rules", lines 132-140.
  - Binary-file skip (extension allowlist + first-512-bytes NUL sniff;
    SVG/Excalidraw deliberately treated as text): lines 141-154.
  - Deterministic ordering on score ties via existing (sortKey, id)
    lexicographic tie-break in sortArtifacts: "### Deterministic ordering
    on score ties", lines 156-164.
  - Upgrade trigger to bleve / sqlite-FTS (10k-artifact corpus, 150 ms p95
    over two consecutive weekly measurements, or 128 MiB scanned bytes per
    query): "### Upgrade trigger to bleve / sqlite-FTS", lines 166-183.

AC2 — Benchmark file compiles, runs, and reports p95 against a
500-artifact fixture (mix of textual + binary, varied sizes):
  File: cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts_bench_test.go
  - BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture (line 29).
  - buildBench500Fixture composes the TD-029 mix: 200 small md, 100 medium
    md, 50 large md, 25 oversize md, 75 PNG, 50 tar (line 94-101).
  - Reports p50_us/op, p95_us/op, p99_us/op via b.ReportMetric (lines
    69-71).

AC3 — Documented p95 budget in TD-NNN matches measured baseline:
  TD-029 "### Performance budget" sets a 200 ms p95 budget and records the
  measured baseline of ~148 ms (lines 200, 214-216). Re-run in this
  worktree on the in-CI runner produced p95 ~155 ms, comfortably within
  the documented 200 ms budget.

AC4 — `cd cli && go test -bench=. -run=^$ ./internal/server/graphql/...`
produces output:
  Run on this worktree:
    BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture-12   2  159347692 ns/op
        155494 p50_us/op  155494 p95_us/op  155494 p99_us/op
    PASS
    ok  github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/server/graphql 1.189s

Conclusion
----------
All four acceptance criteria are met by code already in main. No new
commit is warranted. Reopening this bead would only introduce churn —
the correct close-out is to acknowledge the prior commit
(bc117725) as the bead's deliverable.

data.text:
All four AC items are satisfied by content already merged into main via `bc117725`:

- **TD-029 search semantics** (field precedence, 256 KiB size cap, binary skip, (sortKey, id) tie-break, upgrade thresholds): `docs/helix/02-design/technical-designs/TD-029-artifact-search-and-facet-contract.md` lines 112–183.
- **500-artifact benchmark**: `cli/internal/server/graphql/resolver_artifacts_bench_test.go` (`BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture`).
- **p95 budget vs measured**: 200 ms documented budget; measured ~155 ms on this runner.
- **Bench command produces output**: verified — `BenchmarkArtifactsSearch_500Fixture-12   2  159347692 ns/op  155494 p95_us/op   PASS`.

The prior REVIEW:BLOCK was against a checkpoint slice that didn't include the implementation commit; the implementation itself is in main. Wrote `.ddx/executions/20260502T144707-0fda2da6/no_changes_rationale.txt` documenting the evidence and exiting without a new commit.
